description: Recommended MCP server registry for the game-development scaffold. This file is intentionally configuration-oriented and uses placeholder commands or environment keys where local setup differs by team.

filesystem.purpose: Read and traverse the working repository, generated docs, logs, and local output files.
when_to_use: read generated design or technical docs
notes: Restrict access to the project root or approved workspace directories.
Prefer read-first workflows; write access should be intentional.

git.purpose: Inspect branches, diffs, blame, recent changes, and release history.
when_to_use: prepare release notes or patch notes
notes: Keep repository scope explicit.
Use together with filesystem access for code and content reviews.

github.purpose: Review pull requests, issues, discussions, and remote repository state.
when_to_use: inspect CI status or release metadata
notes: Use least-privilege tokens.
Useful for PR review, release planning, and issue triage agents.

ripgrep.purpose: Fast codebase and content text search for symbols, config keys, and repeated patterns.
notes: Best paired with filesystem and git servers.
Critical for large Unity, Unreal, and Godot repositories.

fetch-docs.purpose: Retrieve official documentation pages, package docs, API references, and public technical references.
notes: Prefer official docs domains in production workflows.
Useful for all engine-specific reviewers and build resolvers.

unity-docs.purpose: Unity scripting, manual, package, and platform-build documentation.
notes: Use for Unity-only sessions and profiles.
Keep package-version context explicit in docs and technical design.

unity-package-registry.purpose: Lookup Unity package metadata, versions, changelogs, and dependency notes.
notes: Useful for build engineers, reviewers, and package governance workflows.

unreal-docs.purpose: Unreal documentation for framework classes, packaging, rendering, networking, and editor workflows.
notes: Use for Unreal-only sessions and profile installs.

godot-docs.purpose: Godot class reference, export docs, scene/node docs, and editor workflow guidance.
notes: Use for Godot-only sessions and profile installs.

issue-tracker.purpose: Read and update backlog items, bugs, milestones, and release tasks in the team issue tracker.
notes: Recommended for producer, QA, release, and doc-updater workflows.
Keep write permissions intentional.

telemetry.purpose: Inspect dashboards, KPI tables, event definitions, and post-launch metrics.
notes: Use for telemetry analysts, liveops managers, and mobile F2P reviews.
Avoid exposing unnecessary player-level data.

fal-media.purpose: Interactive AI asset generation via the fal.ai model catalog: images, skyboxes, 3D models, sound effects, music, speech, and video.
when_to_use: explore or compare generation models before committing them to the provider registry
iterate interactively on prompts before a reproducible generation run
estimate generation cost before batch work
notes: Optional convenience layer. Reproducible production generation goes through scripts/generate-assets.js and manifests/asset-providers.json so provenance sidecars are written.
Follow the AI-generated asset rules in rules/common/asset-pipeline.md regardless of which path generated the file.

blender.purpose: Drive a running Blender instance to inspect scenes and run bpy Python for 3D asset work: build/normalize geometry, set scale/axis/pivot, assign materials, pull PolyHaven/Sketchfab assets, validate against import rules, and export GLB/FBX.
when_to_use: normalize a generated or downloaded model to the project's unit/axis/pivot and naming conventions before it reaches a scene
run the 3d-asset-pipeline import checklist (scale probe, orientation, pivot, polycount) and report pass/fail
kitbash modular pieces with snapping pivots, or pull HDRIs/textures from PolyHaven
clean up and re-export existing rigs/animations (rename actions, trim ranges, fix framerate, assisted retarget)
notes: DCC tool, engine-neutral: it produces GLB/FBX that each engine layer imports under its own rules, so it does not break engine isolation.
Interactive only — needs Blender open with the BlenderMCP addon running (not headless); like fal-media and playwright it is an iteration layer, not a CI/deterministic path.

Scope: geometry, normalization, import validation, and export are solid. Rigging and character animation are only assisted (auto-rig, retarget, clip cleanup) — precise bone placement, weight painting, and keyframe timing still need a human. Do not present it as an autonomous rigger/animator.

Pairs with skills 3d-asset-pipeline, rigging-skinning-pipeline, 3d-animation-pipeline, lighting-lod-pipeline and the /art-3d-pass command; follow rules/common/asset-pipeline.md for any AI-generated source.

unity-editor.purpose: Drive the Unity Editor from the assistant via MCP for Unity (CoplayDev): inspect and modify scenes and GameObjects, manage and import assets, run Editor menu items, read the console, and edit/run C# — so scene scaffolding and audits act directly in the Editor instead of hand-editing YAML.
when_to_use: scaffold or rewire a scene live in the Editor (create GameObjects, set components/references) instead of editing .unity/.prefab YAML by hand
audit scene/prefab structure and serialized references against the unity rules while the project is open
run Editor menu items, trigger imports, or read the console while diagnosing a build or import
apply a unity-setup/scene-bootstrap result directly to the open project
notes: Two halves like the Blender MCP: install the Unity-side bridge in your project (Package Manager -> Add package from git URL: https://github.com/CoplayDev/unity-mcp.git?path=/MCPForUnity#main) and keep the Unity Editor open; the harness launches this server (PyPI package mcpforunityserver) which connects to it.
Prereqs: Unity 2021.3 LTS+, Python 3.10+ with uv (provides uvx). First run downloads mcpforunityserver from PyPI.
Engine-specific (engine-control): use only inside Unity work; it does not apply to Unreal/Godot/web. Keep Unity implementation detail in the Unity rules layer.
Pairs with the unity-setup, scene-bootstrap, unity-scene-audit, and unity-review commands. Distinct from unity-docs / unity-package-registry, which are documentation servers, not Editor control.

godot-editor.purpose: Drive the Godot engine via the Godot MCP server (Coding-Solo): launch the editor, run projects, read/modify scenes and nodes, edit scripts, and capture debug/console output so scene work and run-time debugging act on the real project.
when_to_use: run the project and capture debug output / errors while diagnosing a bug
inspect or modify scene/node structure against the godot rules instead of editing .tscn by hand
apply a godot-setup / scene work result to the open project
launch the editor or a scene for a quick playtest loop
notes: Portable: runs straight from npm (@coding-solo/godot-mcp) via npx, no clone or path needed. GODOT_PATH is optional (auto-detected); set it in your environment only if detection fails.
Engine-specific (engine-control): use only inside Godot work; it does not apply to Unity/Unreal/web. Keep Godot implementation detail in the Godot rules layer.
Pairs with the godot-setup, godot-scene-audit, godot-build-fix, and godot-review commands. Distinct from godot-docs, which is a documentation server, not engine control.

unreal-editor.purpose: Drive the Unreal Editor via the Unreal MCP server (chongdashu): control actors, blueprints, levels, and editor automation so scene/blueprint work acts directly in the Editor.
when_to_use: spawn/edit actors and wire blueprints live instead of by hand
audit level/blueprint structure against the unreal rules while the project is open
run editor automation or read output while diagnosing a build
apply an unreal-setup result to the open project
notes: NOT pre-wired: the canonical server (github.com/chongdashu/unreal-mcp) has no portable npx/uvx package — it launches from a local clone via `uv --directory <path>/Python run unreal_mcp_server.py`, so the path is a per-machine placeholder. Replace it in your own client config; the scaffold never commits a machine path.

Two halves like the other engine servers: install the UnrealMCP plugin (MCPGameProject/Plugins/UnrealMCP) in your project and keep the Editor open; this server connects to it. Prereqs: Python 3.10+ with uv.
Engine-specific (engine-control): use only inside Unreal work; it does not apply to Unity/Godot/web. Keep Unreal implementation detail in the Unreal rules layer.
Pairs with the unreal-setup, unreal-blueprint-audit, unreal-build-fix, and unreal-review commands. Distinct from unreal-docs, which is a documentation server, not Editor control.

playwright.purpose: Drive a real browser to playtest and QA web (HTML5) builds: navigate, click/drag/type, capture screenshots, and read console + page errors.
when_to_use: smoke-test a running web build end to end (boot, start, play, restart)
capture gameplay or menu screenshots for review
verify menu/HUD flows and surface console or page errors
check GPU-memory-flat or perf signals exposed on window during play
notes: Microsoft's official Playwright MCP. First run needs browsers installed: `npx playwright install chromium`.
Point it at a local dev/preview server (e.g. `npm run dev` / `npm run preview`), not a file:// path, so ES modules load.
Optional interactive layer. Each sample still keeps a reproducible smoke path in its own scripts/ (headless sim test + a committed Playwright script); the MCP is for live, ad hoc QA.
Web-target only — it tests browser builds, not the Unity/Unreal/Godot editors (use an engine MCP for those).

storefront-docs.purpose: Review store submission rules, metadata requirements, release checklists, and platform documentation.
notes: Useful for release-manager and console-compliance-reviewer workflows.

ci-builds.purpose: Inspect CI pipelines, build artifacts, test outputs, and packaging logs.
notes: Recommended for build engineers, release managers, and engine build resolvers.
